sweep.rule: A fact is stated only if >=2 independent sources agree. Pending/forward events are kept in the future tense. Every datapoint is tagged sourced or estimated.
sweep.note: Refreshed on the 30-Jun-2026 (Tuesday) sweep — month-end, quarter-end and half-year-end. The big shift from the weekend's war scare is a RISK-ON RELIEF RALLY. The US-Iran 'stand down for now' framework held over the weekend, Strait-of-Hormuz vessels are moving freely again, and the two sides meet in Doha TODAY (Tuesday 30-Jun; Witkoff + Kushner) for technical talks on the MoU — the war premium has largely bled back out. Monday's tape ripped higher on the de-escalation plus a Magnificent-Seven rebound: the Dow closed at a record 52,182.74 (+0.59%), the S&P 500 +1.18% to 7,440.43 and the Nasdaq +2.07% to 25,820.14, reversing most of the prior week's AI/semis sell-off (NVDA bounced off ~$193, AVGO ~$370, Alphabet +4% on its Dow debut). VIX eased to 18.41. Rates barely moved: the 10Y sits ~4.37-4.39% after May PCE (25-Jun) printed broadly in line at 4.1% headline / 3.4% core, nudging the market to scale back the magnitude of 2026 Fed hikes (still ~62% odds of a September move off the 17-Jun hawkish hold at 3.50-3.75%). FX: DXY held ~101.3 (off the 14-month high of 101.8 on 24-Jun) ahead of payrolls and a midweek ECB forum where the Fed Chair speaks; EUR/USD ~1.13-1.14, GBP/USD ~1.32 (near a 7-month low on a soft UK composite PMI of 49.4); USD/JPY pinned ~161.9 — fresh 40-year highs, the weakest yen since 1986, deep in MoF-intervention territory, BoJ next 31-Jul. Commodities diverged: Brent rebounded to ~$73-74 (from ~$72 Friday, its lowest since 27-Feb) as the weekend strikes briefly revived the tail, while gold SLIPPED to ~$4,050 — the dollar is winning the safe-haven bid and bullion is on track for a fourth straight monthly loss (~-10% on the month, ~27% below the Jan record). Bitcoin ~$59.8k (~-18% on the month). Earnings: Micron's record FQ3 (24-Jun) and FedEx's beat-but-sold-off FQ4 (23-Jun) are now priced and several days old; Nike FQ4 prints AFTER THE CLOSE TODAY (30-Jun, ~1:15pm PT). Implied moves are options-desk estimates (labelled estimated); consensus figures are flagged where not dual-confirmed.

earnings.tradeStatement: Stay long Micron (MU) but express it with discipline six days after the record FQ3 — rev $41.46B, EPS $25.11 and a ~$50B Q4 guide (GM ~86%) drove the stock up ~15% post-print toward ~$1,200, so the HBM/AI-memory thesis is now confirmed AND priced; for concentrated holders monetise the gap with a zero-cost collar or call-overwrite rather than chasing, even as the broader AI/semis complex bounced back Monday in the relief rally.
earnings.name: Micron Technology
earnings.headline: Micron's record blow-out is confirmed and priced — and the AI/semis tape just snapped back to records.
earnings.thesis: Micron reported FQ3 (ended 28-May) after the close on 24 June and it was the print of the quarter: record revenue $41.46B (beating ~$35.25B consensus), non-GAAP EPS $25.11 (vs ~$20.28), and a Q4 guide of ~$50B ±$1B (~86% gross margin). HBM3E/HBM4 are sold out through calendar 2027 with demand into 2028; HBM4 12-high is ramping ~2x faster than HBM3E. The stock gapped ~15% post-print toward ~$1,200 (Goldman lifted its target to $1,100). Six sessions on, the 2027 pricing thesis is confirmed but largely in the price — and it now sits inside an AI/semis complex that just rebounded hard (Nasdaq +2.07% Monday, NVDA off ~$193, AVGO ~$370) as the ceasefire held and the Mag7 led a relief rally. This is no longer a 'buy the event' setup; it is a 'how do I hold a name at an all-time high' problem. For concentrated holders, monetise the move with a collar or overwrite; for others, add on pullbacks, not into strength.

variant.street: Analysts raised targets across the board on the record print and ~$50B guide (Goldman to $1,100)
variant.us: Thesis confirmed but largely priced after a ~15% gap to ATHs — hold via a collar/overwrite, add only on dips even as the semis tape rebounds
variant.gap: The crowd chases the gap; the edge now is in the expression (monetise the move) rather than the direction

earnings.changeMyMind: Guidance or channel checks pointing to 2027 HBM pricing rolling over, or the booked-capacity contracts being renegotiated — the 2027 pricing path, not the quarter just reported, is the thesis.

pillars.note: A ~15% gap to all-time highs on a blow-out leaves little clean edge chasing — the reaction is broadly proportionate to the print.
Unanimously bullish sell-side; Goldman raised its target to $1,100 from $900 and the cohort lifted estimates on the ~$50B guide.
Dated, landed blow-out (24 Jun) — record revenue and a guide-up that re-rated the name.
Crowded long at all-time highs after the gap, now into a rebounding semis tape — momentum, not a washed-out or short-squeeze setup.

earnings.result: Blow-out beat — record revenue $41.46B (vs ~$35.25B est), non-GAAP EPS $25.11 (vs ~$20.28); Q4 guided to ~$50B ±$1B with gross margin ~86%; HBM sold out through CY2027 into 2028
earnings.reaction: Shares rose ~15% in extended trade after the print toward ~$1,200 (off a 24-Jun close ~$1,211); Goldman raised its price target to $1,100 from $900
earnings.watch: 2027 HBM pricing durability, the HBM4 ramp, and whether the name extends with the broader AI/semis complex now that the tape has snapped back.

facts.text: Micron reported FQ3 FY26 (ended 28-May) on 24 Jun: record revenue $41.46B (beat ~$35.25B), non-GAAP EPS $25.11 (vs ~$20.28)
Q4 guided to ~$50B ±$1B with gross margin ~86%; HBM3E/HBM4 booked through CY2027, demand into 2028; shares rose ~15% post-print toward ~$1,200
The wider AI/semis tape rebounded Monday — Nasdaq +2.07% to 25,820, NVDA off ~$193, AVGO ~$370 — in a Mag7-led relief rally as the ceasefire held

earnings.tradeStatement: Don't reflexively buy the FedEx (FDX) dip — FQ4 beat ($6.31 EPS on $25.0B) but the stock fell ~6% under $300 because the first post-Freight-spin-off guide for CY2026 ($16.90-18.10) reads as a cautious transition year; with the global-trade bellwether flashing caution, play the reaction with defined risk (buffered note or wait), not a naked add — even as Hormuz shipping risk eases into today's Doha talks.
earnings.name: FedEx
earnings.headline: FedEx beat but fell ~6% on a cautious first post-spin-off guide — the signal, not the dip.
earnings.thesis: FedEx reported FQ4 after the close on 23 June: adjusted EPS $6.31 on revenue $25.0B, beating ~$5.91 / ~$24.2B, having completed the FedEx Freight spin-off (FDXF began trading 1 June). Yet the stock fell ~6% and slipped back under $300 (from near a 52-week high ~$336) — the first standalone CY2026 guide ($16.90-18.10 adjusted EPS, ~11% consolidated revenue growth) reads as a cautious, cost-pressured transition year. Unlike a clean capex-overshoot dip, this selloff reflects genuinely soft forward guidance from the global-trade / industrial bellwether — a yellow flag for the 'broadening beyond mega-caps' rotation. The weekend's Hormuz tail has since eased (vessels moving freely, Doha talks today), removing one near-term trade-route overhang, but the transition-year caution is the durable signal. Play the reaction with discipline: a defined-risk re-entry (buffered note) or wait for the dust to settle, rather than chasing the bounce.

variant.street: Bulls point to the beat, the cheap multiple and the spin-off unlocking value
variant.us: The cautious transition-year guide from the trade bellwether is the signal — wait, don't chase the dip
variant.gap: Market may under-weight how a soft freight/industrial guide reads through to the broadening-rotation thesis

earnings.changeMyMind: A re-acceleration in freight demand or DRIVE/Network 2.0 cost savings clearly beating the cautious guide — evidence the transition year is better than feared turns the wait into an add.

pillars.note: A beat that sold off ~6% offers a dip, but the cautious forward guide is legitimate — not a clean misprice.
Cheap multiple but ratings mixed; the soft transition-year guide splits the Street.
Dated, landed reaction (23 Jun) — a beat overshadowed by cautious guidance and spin-off transition noise.
Cyclical and sentiment-sensitive, but not at a positioning extreme after the drop.

earnings.result: Beat — adjusted EPS $6.31 on revenue $25.0B (vs ~$5.91 / ~$24.2B); completed the FedEx Freight spin-off (FDXF listed 1 Jun); CY2026 guided to $16.90-18.10 adjusted EPS on ~11% consolidated revenue growth
earnings.reaction: Shares fell ~6% after the print and slipped back under $300 (from near a 52-week high ~$336) on the cautious transition-year guide and spin-off cost pressures
earnings.watch: Freight-demand trajectory, Network 2.0 / DRIVE cost savings, tariff and trade-route drag, and whether the cautious CY2026 guide proves conservative.

facts.text: FedEx reported FQ4 FY26 on 23 Jun: adjusted EPS $6.31 on revenue $25.0B, beating ~$5.91 / ~$24.2B; the FedEx Freight spin-off (FDXF) completed and began trading 1 Jun
CY2026 guided to $16.90-18.10 adjusted EPS on ~11% consolidated revenue growth — a cautious first standalone guide
Shares fell ~6% and slipped back under $300, from near a 52-week high ~$336, on the transition-year guide

earnings.tradeStatement: Long Nike (NKE) into FQ4 after the close TODAY (30 June) — a single-name turnaround bet (the 'Win Now' reset, China inventory clear-down, a one-off tariff-refund gain) priced for bad news with the Street braced for an EPS down ~21% YoY; express it with a structure (reverse convertible / cash-secured puts) given a low bar and an eight-quarter beat streak, not naked stock.
earnings.name: Nike
earnings.headline: Nike FQ4 after the close TODAY (30 June) — a low-bar turnaround print into a 'better-than-feared' setup
earnings.thesis: Nike reports fiscal Q4 after the close on Tuesday 30 June (results ~1:15pm PT, call ~2pm PT), with consensus around $0.11 EPS (a steep ~21% YoY decline from $0.14) on ~$10.85B revenue (~-3% YoY, inside management's -2% to -4% guide) — a deliberately low bar. This is a company-specific turnaround: CEO Elliott Hill's 'Win Now' reset is showing early stabilisation in wholesale but not yet the revenue or margin inflection that justifies multiple expansion, and management is steering a self-imposed ~20% China revenue decline in Q4 to clear inventory. A one-off tariff-refund gain is expected to flatter the headline; ex that, the quarter should land close to prior guidance. Nike has beaten EPS in each of the last eight quarters, so the bar is low and the print is a 'better-than-feared' setup — off the desk's strategic themes but a real dated catalyst for any book with consumer-discretionary exposure, best expressed with a defined-risk structure.
earnings.offThemeWhy: No standing desk theme on consumer discretionary — this is a single-name turnaround catalyst, judged on its own merit, not a strategic house view.
earnings.changeMyMind: China decline failing to stabilise or gross margins falling further ex-tariff-refund — the turnaround thesis depends on China bottoming and margin inflecting, not a one-off refund.

pillars.note: Implied ~8% vs ~7% realised — modestly rich for a turnaround print, but the low bar caps the asymmetry.
Street cautious — EPS down ~21% YoY and revenue lower, the turnaround running slow; no clean buy-side alignment.
Dated FQ4 after the close TODAY (30 Jun) — a real, scheduled single-name catalyst.
Beaten-down name with a low bar, an eight-quarter beat streak and some short interest, but the slow turnaround keeps it from a clean squeeze.

earnings.watch: China revenue trajectory (managed ~-20%), gross-margin recovery ex the tariff-refund one-off, wholesale re-stocking, and the FY27 guide.

facts.text: Nike reports FQ4 fiscal-2026 results after the close TODAY 30 June (~1:15pm PT); consensus ~$0.11 EPS (down ~21% from $0.14 a year ago) on ~$10.85B revenue (~-3% YoY, inside the -2% to -4% guide)

Management is steering a self-imposed ~20% China revenue decline in Q4 to clear inventory; a one-off tariff-refund gain is expected to flatter the headline; Nike has beaten EPS in each of the last eight quarters

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long gold (XAU) — accumulate ~$4,050 as strategic ballast; a bet on continued central-bank buying and currency debasement, even as a firm dollar wins the near-term safe-haven bid and bullion grinds out a fourth straight monthly loss — sized to the protection gap, not a tactical momentum trade.
exEarnings.name: Gold — accumulate the pullback
exEarnings.headline: Gold slips to ~$4,050 as the dollar wins the haven bid — a deeper accumulation window
exEarnings.thesis: Gold has slipped to around $4,050 — roughly 27% below its January record — snapping a two-day rebound as the firm dollar absorbed the safe-haven flows that bullion would normally capture, and is now on track for a fourth straight monthly loss (~-10% on the month) with the hawkish Fed, a 4.1% PCE and DXY ~101.3 capping it. The strategic case is unchanged: central banks stay net buyers and every major bank's year-end target sits well above spot. For under-protected books this dollar-driven dip is exactly the accumulation window in the cleanest debasement-and-geopolitics hedge — sized to the protection gap, bought into weakness rather than chased.

variant.street: Every major bank bullish — year-end targets well above spot; central banks net buyers
variant.us: Same direction — accumulate into under-protected books on the dollar-driven dip, even through a fourth monthly loss

exEarnings.changeMyMind: Central banks turning net sellers for two consecutive quarters, or real yields breaking decisively higher.

pillars.note: Bank targets ~30-50% above spot versus gold's normal trailing move — roughly a 1.5-2x asymmetry to the upside from ~$4,050.
No single dated catalyst — a structural accumulation; near-term the firm dollar and hawkish Fed are a headwind, not a tailwind.
Every major bank's year-end target sits above spot and central banks are net buyers — sell-side and flow both confirm.
Driver specificity yes (sourced central-bank tonnage and named bank targets), but no variant edge — this is the consensus call, not a differentiated one.
Core to the Gold & Currency house view as strategic ballast.
~27% off the highs and into a fourth monthly loss with positioning reset — neither washed-out nor crowded; mild fuel.
Slipped back to ~$4,050 below the January high; the long-term uptrend (above the rising 200-day) is intact but the near-term trend is soft — mixed.

macro.detail: ~27% below the 28-Jan record of $5,589; slipped back to ~$4,050 (snapping a two-day rebound) as the firm dollar won the safe-haven bid and gold headed for a fourth straight monthly loss (~-10% on the month). Central banks net +244t in Q1 and +17t in April; China adding for 18 straight months.
macro.watch: The strong dollar / hawkish Fed path as the near-term headwind; central-bank demand and the geopolitics tail as the marginal buyers underpinning the dip.

facts.text: Gold slipped to ~$4,050 on 29 Jun, about 27% below the 28-Jan all-time high of $5,589, snapping a two-day rebound as the firm dollar absorbed safe-haven flows
Gold is on track for a fourth straight monthly loss, down more than 10% on the month
Central banks net buyers: +244t in Q1 2026, +17t in April; China 18 consecutive months; bank year-end targets Goldman $5,400, JPMorgan ~$6,000, Morgan Stanley $5,200, UBS $5,500

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long the power & grid build-out (utilities, gas, nuclear) — a lower-beta way to be long the AI theme that pays contracted income; a bet on structural datacenter electricity demand, not on interest rates — and a steadier place to sit even as the AI chip complex whipsaws from sell-off to record in a week.
exEarnings.name: AI power & the grid
exEarnings.headline: Electricity is the binding constraint on AI — and the lower-beta way to own it
exEarnings.thesis: Datacenter electricity demand is on track for roughly 1,050 TWh in 2026 — doubling in a few years — with gas and nuclear the dispatchable supply. Owning the power and grid build-out is the lower-beta way to stay long the AI theme, and it pays contracted income while it compounds. The AI chip complex just round-tripped in a week — a -4.6% Nasdaq sell-off then a +2.07% Monday snapback to records — and that volatility is the point: the power bottleneck behind the build-out (and Micron's record HBM print as a reminder the demand keeps scaling) is a steadier expression of the same secular story.

variant.street: Crowds the AI chip names to own the theme
variant.us: Own the power and grid bottleneck instead — lower-beta, contracted income, still under-owned, calmer when chips whipsaw
variant.gap: The market pays up for compute and underprices the electricity constraint behind it

exEarnings.changeMyMind: Datacenter load-growth forecasts being cut, or utility rate-base and PPA approvals stalling.

pillars.note: Datacenter power demand roughly doubling — a large structural move versus the sector's normal drift.
A structural demand story rather than a single dated event.
IEA data and some sell-side support, but no independent breakout / flow confirmation yet.
Quantified driver (IEA: datacenter load ~1,050 TWh, roughly doubling) and a real variant — own the power bottleneck rather than the crowded, more-volatile chips. Driver yes, variant yes.
Core Power & Infrastructure house view.
Still under-owned versus the chip names — the crowd is clearly not yet with us, which is fuel.
Multi-year rate-base and load growth; the trend is constructive but there's no clean breakout off the moving averages yet — mixed.

macro.detail: IEA projects datacenter electricity use approaching ~1,050 TWh by 2026; dispatchable gas and nuclear lead the supply, with SMRs entering after 2030. A lower-beta sleeve while the AI chip complex stays choppy.
macro.watch: Utility rate-base approvals, datacenter PPAs and nuclear/SMR newsflow.

facts.text: IEA: datacenter electricity use approaching ~1,050 TWh by 2026, more than double recent levels
Gas and nuclear are the dispatchable supply; SMRs enter the mix after 2030

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long high-quality duration — extend from cash / bills into 5-10y govt / IG to lock ~4.4% yields; a bet that carry plus eventual cuts beats cash, accepting mark-to-market risk while the Fed stays hawkish — with the 10Y boxed ~4.37-4.39% after an in-line PCE.
exEarnings.name: Quality carry — get paid to wait
exEarnings.headline: PCE ran in line and the Fed stayed hawkish — own the carry; the 10Y sits ~4.37-4.39%
exEarnings.thesis: May PCE (25 June) printed broadly in line at 4.1% headline with core 3.4% — still the hottest core in over two years, but in-line enough to let the market scale back the magnitude of 2026 Fed hikes — and the Fed held at 3.50-3.75% on 17 June with a hawkish dot plot (median still sees hikes, ~62% September odds). The 'extend before the cuts' convexity trade is gone for now; the case left is simpler and still good — you are paid ~4.4% in high-quality intermediate bonds to wait, ballast that cash can't give a book with equities back at records. The 10Y has settled ~4.37-4.39%, little changed through the relief rally, a reminder duration earns its diversification keep without needing a near-term rally. Add for income and diversification, not for a quick gain.

variant.street: The in-line PCE lets yields settle; the Street is split between a 2026 hike and a 2027 pivot
variant.us: Own quality carry now — ~4.4% is paid ballast that rallies in a risk-off; don't bank on near-term cuts (or fear every hike)
variant.gap: The market may still be too optimistic on the timing of the first cut

exEarnings.changeMyMind: Inflation rolling back toward target (pulling the first cut forward), or the Fed actually hiking through 4.5% — either end of the barbell breaks the simple carry case.

pillars.note: Earning ~4.4% with cuts now a 2027 story — a carry trade with limited near-term convexity, a modest expected move versus normal bond ranges.
The 25-Jun PCE landed in line and the 17-Jun Fed held hawkish; the next dated catalyst (Jul payrolls/CPI, late-Jul FOMC) is weeks out — structural, not imminent.
The PCE print is sourced fact, but the Street is split on the path — alignment is weaker than a clean carry consensus.
Quantified, sourced drivers (PCE 4.1% / core 3.4%, hawkish hold, 10Y ~4.37%), but mostly the consensus read — a modest variant on cut timing.
Core Fixed Income house view — quality income and ballast, even with the cut delayed.
The crowd is still parked in cash and bills; extending is the contrarian-leaning move.
10Y holding ~4.37-4.39% through the relief rally; the price trend is mixed — neutral-to-confirming for a carry buyer.

macro.detail: May PCE (25 Jun) printed in line at 4.1% headline, core 3.4% (still the hottest core in two-plus years). The Fed held 3.50-3.75% on 17 Jun with a hawkish dot plot — ~62% September odds, cuts pushed to 2027. The 10Y sits ~4.37-4.39%, little changed through Monday's risk-on relief rally.
macro.watch: The next payrolls/CPI and the late-July FOMC — whether inflation hardens the hawkish path; and whether duration holds its diversification value as equities sit at records.

facts.text: May PCE printed broadly in line at 4.1% y/y headline with core 3.4% (released 25 Jun), letting markets scale back the magnitude of expected 2026 Fed hikes
The Fed held at 3.50-3.75% on 17 Jun with a hawkish dot plot; markets price ~62% odds of a September move, cuts pushed to 2027
The 10Y Treasury yield sits ~4.37-4.39%, little changed through Monday's relief rally

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Buy a ~12-month 10-year Treasury range accrual note — earn an enhanced ~7.5% p.a. coupon (vs ~4.0% on cash) for each day the 10Y yield fixes inside a 4.00-4.90% band; with the 10Y at ~4.37-4.39% you are paid to hold the view that a hawkish-hold Fed keeps the long end boxed below the proven 5% ceiling, forgoing the coupon only on a decisive break above 4.90% or below 4.00%.
exEarnings.name: 10Y range accrual
exEarnings.trigger: The 25-Jun in-line PCE (4.1% headline, core 3.4%) and the 17-Jun hawkish Fed hold (~62% September odds) cap the 10Y from both sides — a sticky-inflation floor that stops it rallying under 4%, a policy path anchored against a runaway break through 5%. Even Monday's risk-on relief rally left the 10Y mid-band at ~4.37-4.39% — exactly the boxed-in regime a range accrual is paid to harvest.
exEarnings.headline: The in-line PCE and hawkish hold box the 10Y below 5% — get paid an enhanced coupon to hold the range
exEarnings.thesis: May PCE printed in line at 4.1% (core 3.4%) on 25 June and the Fed held hawkish on 17 June (~62% September odds) — a stance that boxes in the 10Y from both sides, with the 10Y at ~4.37-4.39% sitting mid-range even through Monday's record-setting equity rally. The TOP of the band is the harder call and the case is specific: 5% has been a proven ceiling — the 10Y peaked near 5.0% in October 2023 and has failed there on every test since, because at ~5% nominal (~2%+ real) duration is the cheapest in two decades and each approach draws heavy real-money buying — pensions and insurers matching liabilities, foreign reserve managers, and asset allocators rotating out of richly-valued equities. The BOTTOM (~4.00%) is held by sticky inflation — PCE at 4.1% — plus no cuts until 2027 to fuel a rally and an elevated term premium on heavy Treasury supply. So a 12-month 10Y range accrual pays an enhanced ~7.5% p.a. coupon (vs ~4.0% on cash) for each day the yield fixes inside 4.00-4.90%: you are paid to hold the boxed-in view, with the coupon forgone only on the days it breaks out. Capital is returned at par at maturity (issuer credit aside) — more than cash without an outright duration bet.

variant.street: Bears see fiscal/supply pushing the 10Y toward 5%; bulls wait for cuts to pull it under 4% — both expect a break
variant.us: Neither break holds: 5% is a proven buyer's ceiling and ~4% a sticky-inflation floor — monetise the range rather than bet on the break
variant.gap: The market pays up for the tail break in either direction and under-prices the 10Y simply staying boxed around 4.4%

exEarnings.changeMyMind: A decisive break of the box — a growth scare or sharp disinflation that pulls the 10Y below 4.00% (cuts brought forward), or a fiscal/supply/inflation shock that drives it through 5.00% — either zeros the accrual.

pillars.note: An enhanced coupon (~7.5% vs ~4% cash) for the 10Y simply staying boxed in — a steady carry pickup with capped, income-style upside rather than an outright duration bet.
Dated, landed catalysts — the 25-Jun in-line PCE and 17-Jun hawkish Fed hold that cap the 10Y from both ends.
The higher-for-longer, range-bound-10Y read is broadly shared, but the precise 4.00-4.90% band and the coupon edge are an estimated structuring call.
Sourced drivers (PCE 4.1% / core 3.4%, hawkish hold, cuts to 2027, 10Y ~4.37%) plus a specific two-sided case: a proven 5% buyer's ceiling above and a sticky-PCE / supply term-premium floor below.
Core Fixed Income / income house view — paid carry from a boxed-in long end, no outright duration call.
The crowd is parked in cash and bills or positioned for a 10Y break either way; selling 10Y rate-range optionality for an enhanced coupon is the contrarian income move.
The 10Y at ~4.37-4.39% mid-range, repeatedly rejected at 5% and bid toward 4% — a well-defined range, the accrual's friend.

macro.metric: PCE 4.1% / core 3.4%; hawkish hold; 10Y ~4.37%, capped at 5%
macro.detail: 12-month 10Y Treasury range accrual: ~4.00-4.90% band, ~7.5% p.a. enhanced coupon vs ~4.0% on cash, A-rated issuer, capital at par. Coupon accrues daily for each in-range fixing; forgone on a break above 4.90% or below 4.00%.
macro.watch: The next payrolls/CPI, the late-July FOMC and Treasury supply/auctions — a shock that drives the 10Y through 5% or under 4% and zeros the accrual.

facts.text: May PCE printed in line at 4.1% headline / core 3.4% (25 Jun); the Fed held hawkish on 17 Jun — ~62% September odds, cuts pushed to 2027
The 10Y Treasury yield sits ~4.37-4.39%; it last peaked near 5.0% in Oct 2023 and has failed at that level on every test since
Sticky PCE at 4.1% and heavy Treasury supply keep an inflation/term premium in long yields, a floor near 4%

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long energy majors (XOM / SHEL) — a tactical hedge that the Hormuz risk premium is under-priced again as Brent bounces to ~$73-74 into today's US-Iran Doha talks; a bet on the re-escalation tail (a fragile, non-enforced ceasefire) held in income-paying majors, not a structural oil-bull call.
exEarnings.name: Energy — tactical on Hormuz risk
exEarnings.headline: Brent bounced to ~$73-74 as the US-Iran tail flickered — and the Doha talks are TODAY
exEarnings.thesis: Brent collapsed to ~$72 on Friday — its lowest since 27-Feb — as Strait-of-Hormuz shipping reopened and the war premium bled out almost entirely, then rebounded to ~$73-74 after the weekend US-Iran strikes briefly revived the tail. The 'stand down for now' framework has held: vessels are moving freely through the strait and the two sides meet in Doha TODAY (Tuesday 30-Jun) for technical talks on the MoU. With only a non-enforced framework in place and the key session live today, energy majors offer a tactical, income-paying hedge against the talks breaking down and the premium snapping back — explicitly off the standing house view, a trade rather than a strategic tilt.
exEarnings.offThemeWhy: There is no standing desk theme on oil — this is a tactical, event-driven idea off live Middle-East supply risk, sized as a hedge, not a strategic house view.

variant.street: Priced most of the war premium out as Hormuz reopened — Brent ~$73-74 reflects a market betting the Doha talks hold
variant.us: Hold a cheap re-escalation hedge — the weekend strikes show the tail is real and the ceasefire is only an unenforced framework
variant.gap: Geopolitical tail risk stays under-priced right as the decisive Doha session lands

exEarnings.changeMyMind: The Doha talks hardening the interim framework into a durable settlement that keeps the Strait of Hormuz open, removing the re-escalation tail.

pillars.note: A ceasefire breakdown could quickly re-add the war premium that just bled out from the ~$126 April peak — a large potential move versus oil's normal range.
Hard, dated catalyst — the US-Iran Doha talks set for TODAY (Tuesday 30-Jun), on the back of the weekend strikes.
Off-theme tactical; no standing sell-side alignment behind it.

Sourced price drivers (Brent ~$72 off the ~$126 April peak, then a bounce to ~$73-74 on the strikes) and a genuine variant — the market under-prices the tail into the decisive Doha session. Driver and variant yes.
Off-theme — explicitly a tactical hedge, not a strategic house view.
The premium has re-priced part-way (Brent back to ~$73-74) and the market leans toward de-escalation into Doha — the contrarian hedge case holds but is no longer at an extreme.
Brent rolled to ~$72 then bounced to ~$73-74 — a contrarian hedge against a tape that has mostly priced out the tail.

macro.detail: Fell to ~$72 on 27-Jun (lowest since 27-Feb) as Hormuz flows reopened and the war premium deflated (~40%+ off the ~$126 April war-peak), then rebounded to ~$73-74 after weekend US-Iran strikes and Iranian retaliation. Vessels move freely; the Doha talks are TODAY (30-Jun) and the framework is non-enforced. Range still headline-driven.
macro.watch: Whether today's Doha talks harden the framework, any Hormuz shipping disruption, and OPEC+ supply signals.

facts.text: Brent fell to ~$72 on 27 Jun, its lowest since 27 Feb, as Strait-of-Hormuz shipping reopened and the war premium bled out (~40%+ off the ~$126 30-Apr peak), then rebounded to ~$73-74
The US and Iran agreed to 'stand down for now' over the weekend with vessels moving freely through Hormuz; Witkoff and Kushner fly to Doha for technical talks on the MoU TODAY (Tuesday 30-Jun)

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Reduce / hedge USD exposure in EUR- and GBP-base books — effectively short the dollar against the home currency — a bet that the post-peak USD weakens into 2026 as the Fed-ECB spread eventually narrows; this is NOT a call on any single pair's spot level, nor specifically a yen- or euro-strength view.
exEarnings.name: Hedge the USD concentration
exEarnings.headline: Dollar holding ~101.3 off a 14-month high — a prime window to hedge USD-heavy non-dollar books
exEarnings.thesis: The dollar pushed to a 14-month high (DXY 101.8 on 24-Jun) on the hawkish Fed before easing to ~101.3, holding there through Monday's risk-on relief rally and ahead of payrolls and a midweek ECB forum where the Fed Chair speaks; EUR/USD sits ~1.13-1.14 and cable ~1.32 — even as every 2026 bank outlook still calls a post-peak dollar and an eventually-narrowing Fed-ECB spread. For EUR- and GBP-base books carrying outsized USD sleeves, this stretched level — with the dollar pausing near the highs and two catalysts (payrolls, the ECB-forum Fed remarks) ahead — is the window to hedge the currency mismatch: currency-hedged share classes for Retail, FX forwards or zero-cost collars for Professionals — sized to the mismatch, not run as a directional bet.

variant.street: Major-bank consensus is a post-peak dollar — more USD weakness into 2026 as the Fed-ECB rate spread narrows
variant.us: Same direction; use the hawkish-driven dollar strength (and the pause near the highs) to put hedges on USD-heavy non-dollar books at a better level
variant.gap: The near-term firm dollar is a hedging gift, not a reason to stay unhedged

exEarnings.changeMyMind: The Fed out-hiking every other central bank on a sustained inflation overshoot — a structurally higher-for-longer dollar would delay the turn the hedge is sized for.

pillars.note: A hedge caps a tail (an adverse FX move on a large USD sleeve) rather than chasing a big gain — modest, defined asymmetry.
Dated and landed: the dollar's push to a 14-month high (24-Jun) now pausing ~101.3 into payrolls and a midweek ECB forum — a concrete window to hedge into.
Bank 2026 outlooks broadly agree on a post-peak dollar and a narrowing Fed-ECB spread; the firm spot is the confirming signal.
Specific driver (a book's non-base USD %) and a clear variant (hedge into the firmness), but the entry timing is a soft, estimated call.
Direct expression of the Gold & Currency house view — currency diversification for concentrated-USD books.
Dollar longs are crowded after the hawkish repricing — hedging when the crowd is max-long USD is reasonable timing, not an extreme.
DXY off a 14-month high to ~101.3 — mixed; a level to fade rather than chase.

macro.detail: The dollar pushed to a 14-month high (DXY 101.8 on 24-Jun) on the hawkish Fed before easing to ~101.3, holding there Monday through the relief rally and ahead of payrolls and a midweek ECB forum (EUR/USD ~1.13-1.14, GBP/USD ~1.32); 2026 bank outlooks still describe a post-peak dollar with the Fed-ECB spread set to narrow. USD/JPY remains ~161.9 on the wide differential.
macro.watch: The payrolls/CPI path, the Fed Chair's midweek ECB-forum remarks, and the Fed-ECB / BOJ rate spreads; any intervention-driven or risk-on turn that tops the dollar.

facts.text: Major-bank 2026 FX outlooks describe a post-peak dollar, with the Fed-ECB rate spread set to narrow and EUR/USD seen recovering into 2026

The dollar is extended versus the rest-of-year forecast range, even after pausing near the highs

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Earn the carry on the wide US rate differential — effectively long the high-yielding USD versus a low-yielder (JPY / EUR) via dual-currency deposits — a bet that the differential persists and the pair stays range-bound so you collect the coupon; it is NOT a directional spot view, and the worst case is converting into the currency you'd have bought anyway.
exEarnings.name: FX carry & dual-currency income
exEarnings.headline: Monetise the wide rate differential — FX carry and dual-currency income
exEarnings.thesis: With the front end held high by the hawkish Fed and rate differentials wide, income books can be paid to express a range view on pairs they are happy to hold either side of. Dual-currency deposits and currency-hedged carry turn the differential into an enhanced coupon — you accept conversion at a set rate in return for yield. A Professional, range-bound income tool, not a directional dollar bet — and at 40-year-high USD/JPY (~161.9) with live MoF-intervention risk and the BoJ next meeting 31-Jul, size the conversion strikes with care.

variant.street: Differentials favour the dollar now; a yen and euro recovery is a 2026 story as the gap narrows
variant.us: Earn the carry while the differential is wide, on pairs you would hold either side of
variant.gap: Get paid to wait for the FX moves the house expects later

exEarnings.changeMyMind: A fast, disorderly dollar reversal — e.g. a large MoF intervention or an abrupt dovish Fed pivot — that compresses the differential and collapses the carry.

pillars.note: Carry is steady coupon income with a defined conversion risk — low, bounded asymmetry by design.
Soft and ongoing: the hawkish hold keeps the front end and the differential wide, but there is no single dated trigger.
The wide US rate differential is a hard, confirmable macro fact and the income angle sits squarely in the house view.
Specific driver (the rate gap) and a clear variant (get paid to wait), but the entry and pair selection are soft, estimated calls.
On the Gold & Currency house view — currency diversification and income from the rate differential.
Carry trades are well-populated and crowded at the highs — not at a clear unwind extreme, but intervention risk argues for care.
USD/JPY trends with the differential at 40-year highs — constructive for carry but stretched into intervention territory; mixed.

macro.watch: BOJ/MoF intervention and the ECB path versus the Fed; any sharp differential compression or disorderly intervention that erodes the carry.

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long short-dated USD/JPY puts — buy ~158-strike 4-6wk puts (~1% premium) to play a yen-strength snapback off MoF intervention. Entry on a confirmed stall/reversal ~161, take profit / unwind into 156-157, cut if spot breaks fresh highs above ~162.5.

USD/JPY pressed fresh 40-year highs ~161.9 — weakest yen since 1986 — with no confirmed intervention-driven reversal, and Monday's risk-on relief rally only reinforces the carry. Spot is making highs, not snapping back. The two-sided strangle is the right way to own the intervention binary here, not directional puts. Held back.

exEarnings.headline: USD/JPY at 40-year highs — directional yen-strength puts have no live trigger; own the two-sided strangle instead
exEarnings.thesis: USD/JPY sits at 40-year highs around 161.9, deep in MoF/BoJ intervention territory, held up by a wide US-Japan differential after the in-line PCE and hawkish Fed and reinforced by Monday's risk-on tape. A one-way short-dated put trade is a fast yen-strength snapback bet — but with spot still making highs and the carry dominant, there is no live justification to own directional downside here. This trade only re-arms if intervention actually stalls and reverses spot (a confirmed lower high). Until then it is held back in favour of the two-sided strangle.
exEarnings.offThemeWhy: Tactical, event-driven FX trade around intervention risk — a short-dated directional position, not the desk's strategic currency-diversification house view.

pillars.note: Short-dated puts near an intervention threshold offer a large potential move for ~1% premium — strong, defined asymmetry, but only once the move turns.
Live intervention risk at 40-year highs — a hard catalyst, but spot is still making highs with no confirmed reversal.
Street split — carry longs vs intervention watchers; no clean alignment.
Specific drivers (intervention zone, BoJ hike bias) and a clear variant (complacency on intervention).
Adjacent to the post-peak-dollar lean, but expressed as a tactical trade rather than the strategic view.
Yen shorts are crowded at the highs — offside is fuel, but not a confirmed wash-out signal.
Spot at fresh 40-year highs — a one-way directional fade is premature; constructive only on a confirmed reversal.

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long a EUR/USD call spread — buy 1.18 / sell 1.21 calls, 4-6wk (~0.7% net) to play euro upside as the Fed-ECB gap eventually narrows. Entry ~1.17, target 1.20-1.21, max loss = the ~0.7% net premium; thesis fails if a hawkish-Fed risk-off keeps EUR/USD below ~1.155.
exEarnings.name: EUR/USD upside
exEarnings.trigger: Still not met on the 30-Jun tape: this bullish call spread needs EUR/USD to break and hold above ~1.17 on a softer dollar, but spot is ~1.13-1.14 with DXY holding ~101.3 near a 14-month high. A midweek ECB forum where the Fed Chair could walk back the hawkish signals is the potential arming catalyst — but it hasn't happened, and Monday's relief rally did not turn the dollar. The post-ECB euro catch-up is on hold until the dollar breaks; no live justification today.

levels.stop: max loss = ~0.7% net premium; fails below ~1.13

exEarnings.headline: ECB at 2.25% but the dollar is firm — a EUR/USD call spread that needs a dollar turn to arm
exEarnings.thesis: The ECB raised its policy rate to 2.25% on 11 June while the Fed's hawkish hold keeps it on pause — the Fed-ECB spread is set to narrow eventually, the core of the post-peak-dollar / euro-recovery view. But near-term the in-line PCE and a dollar holding ~101.3 (off a 14-month high) have EUR/USD pinned ~1.13-1.14. A 1.18/1.21 call spread is the cheap, capped way to own the eventual catch-up — but it only arms once EUR/USD reclaims ~1.17 on a softening dollar, the catalyst for which could be the Fed Chair's midweek ECB-forum remarks. Until then, held back.
exEarnings.offThemeWhy: Tactical post-ECB directional trade — a short-dated, defined-risk expression of the euro-recovery lean, not the standing house view.

variant.street: Bulls see a narrowing Fed-ECB spread lifting EUR/USD into 2026

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Bullish GBP/USD via a risk reversal — sell the 1.31 put / buy the 1.38 call, 4-6wk (~zero cost) to lever cable upside on a BoE hold at 3.75% and a soft post-peak dollar. Entry on a break above ~1.355, target 1.38-1.39, unwind/cut if spot breaks 1.315 (you are put the pair at the short strike).
exEarnings.name: GBP/USD upside
exEarnings.trigger: Still offside on the 30-Jun tape: this bullish risk reversal needs cable to break and hold above ~1.355, but GBP/USD is stuck ~1.32 — near a 7-month low — after a soft UK composite PMI (49.4, a second month of contraction) and with DXY holding ~101.3. Monday's relief rally did not lift cable through the trigger, and the 1.31 short put sits uncomfortably close to spot. No live justification — held back.

levels.entry: ~1.32 (well below the 1.355 trigger; short 1.31 put near spot)

exEarnings.headline: Cable stuck ~1.32 near a 7-month low — the bullish risk reversal is below its trigger, held back
exEarnings.thesis: GBP/USD is stuck around 1.32 — near a 7-month low — with the Bank of England holding Bank Rate at 3.75% but UK data softening (June composite PMI 49.4, a second month of contraction) and the dollar firm. The bullish lean (a relatively high carry against a Fed on pause and an eventual post-peak dollar) is intact strategically, but the trade — sell the 1.31 put to fund the 1.38 call — only arms on a confirmed break above ~1.355. With cable near 7-month lows and the 1.31 short strike close to spot, the assignment risk is live and the upside trigger is far off. Held back until the dollar turns and cable reclaims 1.355.
exEarnings.offThemeWhy: Tactical, carry-supported cable trade — a short-dated geared expression, not the strategic currency house view.

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long USD/JPY volatility at the intervention line — buy a 2-4wk strangle (~158 put / ~165 call, ~1.3% total premium) to own the next move either way: a suspected/confirmed MoF intervention or a policy shock could snap it sharply lower, while a dominant carry trade could squeeze it higher through 163. Entry ~161.9, take profit on a break beyond 158 or 165, let it decay / cut if pinned 160-163 (theta).
exEarnings.name: USD/JPY event volatility
exEarnings.trigger: Live on the 30-Jun tape: USD/JPY pressed fresh 40-year highs ~161.9 — the weakest yen since 1986 and deep in MoF-intervention territory — even through Monday's risk-on relief rally. With the BoJ hike-biased into its 31-Jul meeting, MoF-intervention risk live at these levels, the carry still dominant, and month-/quarter-end rebalancing flows in play, the next move is a genuinely two-sided binary — a further carry-driven melt-up vs a sharp intervention snapback. Owning the move either way has a live justification today.
exEarnings.headline: USD/JPY ~161.9 at 40-year highs with live intervention risk — own the two-sided move with a short-dated strangle
exEarnings.thesis: USD/JPY pressed ~161.9 — fresh 40-year highs, the weakest yen since 1986 and squarely in MoF-intervention territory — with officials having signalled they will 'act if necessary' and the wide differential keeping the carry trade dominant and the BoJ hike-biased into its 31-Jul meeting. The next move is genuinely two-sided: fresh intervention or a policy shock could snap it sharply lower, while if the carry keeps winning it squeezes higher through 163 — and month-/quarter-end rebalancing flows add to the swing factor. When you are confident in a big move but not its sign, own the volatility — a 2-4wk strangle (~158 put / ~165 call) profits on a break beyond either wing, with the two premiums as the defined max loss. Take profit on a break beyond 158 or 165; let it decay/expire if spot stays pinned 160-163.
exEarnings.offThemeWhy: Tactical long-volatility trade around live intervention risk at the highs — non-directional, not the strategic currency house view.

variant.street: Carry crowd stays directionally long USD/JPY; implied vol underprices the intervention snapback
variant.us: Own the move either way — the sign is genuinely two-sided at the intervention line, the size is not
variant.gap: Event vol looks cheap relative to the range of plausible intervention and squeeze outcomes

exEarnings.changeMyMind: Spot grinding quietly in 160-163 with no intervention follow-through, no fresh carry leg and a calm backdrop into 31-Jul — theta erodes both premiums.

pillars.note: A binary intervention/squeeze outcome can drive a large move either way for a defined double-premium — strong long-vol asymmetry.
Hard, live trigger — spot at 40-year highs ~161.9 with live intervention risk, the BoJ 31-Jul meeting ahead and month-/quarter-end flows, a two-sided binary now.
Street positions directionally (carry-long); no consensus on the size or timing of the snapback.
Clear long-vol rationale, but the edge depends on implied vol being cheap — a soft, estimated call.
Adjacent to the currency house view, expressed as event vol.
Crowded directional positioning raises the odds of an outsized unwind — supportive of long vol.

macro.detail: Buy a 2-4wk ~158 put / ~165 call strangle (~1.3% total). Take profit beyond 158 or 165; max loss = both premiums if pinned 160-163. Driver: a two-sided binary — intervention snapback vs a carry-driven squeeze higher, with the BoJ 31-Jul meeting and month-end flows in play.
macro.watch: MoF intervention and jawboning, the US-Japan differential, the BoJ 31-Jul meeting, month-/quarter-end flows, and realised vs implied vol.

facts.text: USD/JPY pressed ~161.9 on 29-30 Jun, fresh 40-year highs and the weakest yen since 1986, deep in MoF-intervention territory
MoF-intervention risk is live and the BoJ stays hike-biased into its 31-Jul meeting, yet the wide US-Japan differential keeps the carry dominant — a genuinely two-sided setup
Month-/quarter-end and half-year-end rebalancing flows add a swing factor on top of the intervention binary
